header.title: Daily FI Tape
header.as_of_note: NY close briefing; Asia and pre-market update remains separate.

bullets: Oil fell and the market removed some Fed-hike pressure, producing a bull rally in Treasuries, stronger equities, lower breakevens, and better credit total returns despite still-visible inflation and supply risks.
overall_summary.ny_close_causal_narrative: Oil fell and the market removed some Fed-hike pressure, producing a bull rally in Treasuries, stronger equities, lower breakevens, and better credit total returns despite still-visible inflation and supply risks.

top_drivers.title: Oil relief pulled Fed-hike pricing lower
top_drivers.short_summary: WTI and Brent fell nearly 3%, pushing the market away from a fully priced December hike and toward later policy risk.
top_drivers.detail: UST2Y fell to 4.05%, UST5Y to 4.18%, UST10Y to 4.45%, and BE10Y dropped 5 bp to 229 bp.

top_drivers.title: Duration rallied despite a soft 30-year auction
top_drivers.short_summary: A weak 30-year sale briefly pressured yields, but the oil-driven inflation relief dominated into the close.
top_drivers.detail: UST30Y still fell 8 bp to 4.95%, showing the session was macro-driven rather than clean supply absorption.

top_drivers.title: Risk assets reopened but credit quality stayed tiered
top_drivers.short_summary: SPX, NDX, and SOX rallied sharply while credit ETFs rose, but AI financing and weaker issuers still showed dispersion.
top_drivers.detail: Hanuman Wind postponed a junk bond again, while Danantara and high-quality AI-linked or bank borrowers retained access.

latest_asia_premarket.summary: Separately, checked Asia/pre-market pricing before the Taipei handoff shows 10Y Treasury note futures 109.67 (-0.24%); 30Y Treasury bond futures 112.62 (-0.5%); S&P 500 futures 7,411 (+0.21%); Nasdaq 100 futures 29,566 (+0.34%); WTI crude futures 86.36 (-1.54%).

items.title: 10Y Treasury note futures
items.detail: 109.67, -0.24% from the prior close. Treasury futures give the cleanest early read on whether the duration bid held after the NY close

items.title: 30Y Treasury bond futures
items.detail: 112.62, -0.5% from the prior close. Long-bond futures help separate a persistent long-end bid from a one-session cash-market move

items.title: S&P 500 futures
items.detail: 7,411, +0.21% from the prior close. Equity futures show whether the overnight handoff confirmed or faded the NY risk tone

items.title: Nasdaq 100 futures
items.detail: 29,566, +0.34% from the prior close. Nasdaq futures are the early check on whether growth and technology beta stabilized

items.title: WTI crude futures
items.detail: 86.36, -1.54% from the prior close. WTI futures test whether the oil channel was still easing inflation pressure by the Taipei handoff

watch_events.title: Fed meeting communication
watch_events.why_it_matters: Whether Warsh validates the oil-relief rally or keeps a hawkish bias around sticky inflation.

watch_events.title: ECB follow-through
watch_events.why_it_matters: The ECB's first hike since 2023 made Europe a live test of energy shock pass-through.

watch_events.title: Credit issuance quality
watch_events.why_it_matters: Strong primary demand needs to be separated from governance, leverage, and refinancing stress.

cross_asset_cards.title: Oil
cross_asset_cards.summary: WTI was -2.58% on the day and -5.73% over one week; Brent moved in the same direction, pulling inflation compensation lower and reinforcing Treasury demand.

cross_asset_cards.title: Equities
cross_asset_cards.summary: SPX was +1.75%, NDX +3.29%, and SOX +7.91%; the equity tape leaned tech-beta downside rather than broad risk-appetite confirmation.

cross_asset_cards.title: Credit
cross_asset_cards.summary: LQD was +0.85%, HYG +0.59%, and EMB +0.96%; these are ETF total returns, not direct OAS or CDX. With Treasury yields lower, duration should have helped bond ETF prices, so flat or negative HY/EM returns argue for weak spread confirmation rather than a broad credit rally.

cross_asset_cards.title: Dollar / gold
cross_asset_cards.summary: DXY was -0.09% while gold was -0.44%, consistent with lower yields and limited haven stress.

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening fixed-income thesis

The June 11 close was a duration relief rally led by oil, not a simple growth scare. WTI fell to about 87.71, down 2.58%, and Brent fell to about 90.38, down 2.92%. That decline reduced the urgency of the inflation shock and allowed Treasuries to rally across the curve. UST2Y fell 8 bp to 4.05%, UST5Y fell 9 bp to 4.18%, UST10Y fell 10 bp to 4.45%, and UST30Y fell 8 bp to 4.95%. BE10Y fell 5 bp to about 229 bp and 10Y TIPS fell 5 bp to about 2.16%. The market was therefore repricing both inflation compensation and real-rate pressure. The move was large enough to change the tone from the prior day: the Fed was still hawkishly constrained, but the market no longer treated a December hike as inevitable.

The cross-asset confirmation was strong. SPX rose 1.75%, NDX rose 3.29%, and SOX jumped 7.91%. VIX fell 12.51%. LQD rose 0.85%, HYG 0.59%, JNK 0.57%, and EMB 0.96%. Those credit ETF gains were supported by falling Treasury yields, so they should not be described as pure spread tightening. Still, the combination of lower oil, lower breakevens, higher equities, and lower volatility makes the risk tone materially better than June 10. The market was effectively saying that oil relief can reopen duration and risk appetite at the same time, provided the Fed does not immediately push back.

The day's news explains why that relief had limits. Short-term rates pricing had moved away from a fully priced December hike after Trump said he was cancelling planned strikes and that a time and place for an agreement would be announced. But inflation data were not cleanly dovish: producer-price figures were hotter than expected, and the Fed meeting under new Chair Kevin Warsh was still approaching. The day was therefore not a pivot to cuts. It was a repricing of the oil tail. If the war premium receded, the Fed could wait longer. If oil rose again, the hike debate would return quickly.

Outside the US, the ECB made the global inflation problem explicit. It was set to raise, and then did raise, rates to 2.25% in response to the Iran-war energy shock, becoming the first major central bank to hike because of that conflict. That was the opposite of a universal dovish message. Europe was saying energy pass-through had become broad enough to require action. South Africa had similar concerns after its May hike. Indonesia was allowing yields to rise to defend the rupiah. Thailand's curve had steepened enough to draw long-end interest, but that interest came from valuation after a sharp selloff. The global message was relief at the margin, not an end to the tightening problem.

Credit also improved unevenly. Risk appetite was better, but weaker borrowers did not suddenly become stronger. Hanuman Wind postponed an offshore junk-bond offering for a second time because governance concerns outweighed a mid-to-high 9% yield. At the other end, Danantara completed a $1.5 billion debut dollar deal and both tranches traded higher. AI-linked financing demand was intense, including the proposed disclosure debate around large AI exposures and a $35 billion Anthropic infrastructure financing package. The close therefore deserves a constructive summary, but with a precise caveat: oil relief bought duration and risk assets time; it did not remove the need to discriminate across borrowers, currencies, and central-bank reaction functions.

## Rates, curve, and Fed pricing

Rates traded like oil had shifted the near-term Fed distribution. The biggest Treasury moves came in the 5Y to 10Y sector, with UST5Y down 9 bp and UST10Y down 10 bp. UST2Y fell 8 bp, so the front end participated, but the 2s10s curve actually flattened by about 2 bp to 40 bp. The move says the market reduced the chance that the Fed would need to hike quickly in response to energy, while still preserving a restrictive policy baseline. BE10Y's 5 bp decline was the cleanest expression of the oil channel. TIPS10Y also fell 5 bp, meaning real yields joined the move rather than leaving the rally entirely in inflation compensation.

The local rates story centered on short-term contracts shifting away from a December hike that had been fully priced. The initial trigger was a sharp fall in oil after the president said planned strikes were being cancelled and an agreement timing was being set. The rally overcame a brief setback from weak 30-year auction demand. CreditSights' Zach Griffiths described it as a large duration rally despite a soft auction. That quote matters because it separates the macro impulse from the supply impulse. The auction was not a bullish supply signal. The market rallied because the oil path had changed the inflation and Fed reaction function.

The Fed was still not dovish. Producer prices had risen more than expected, and the upcoming meeting was Warsh's first test as chair. Pimco advisers emphasized that investors were trying to understand how Warsh would communicate, especially because he had previously expressed skepticism about forward guidance. The rates market therefore had to price both the near-term relief from lower oil and the regime uncertainty around Fed communication. That is why the correct conclusion is not that hikes were gone. The correct conclusion is that the market had moved the first fully priced hike further out and demanded confirmation from the Fed.

The real-yield and breakeven mix was healthier than the prior day. BE10Y fell to 229 bp, down 7 bp on the week and 18 bp on the month. TIPS10Y remained elevated at 2.16%, still up 21 bp on the month, so the real-rate burden on risk assets did not vanish. But the one-day move removed the most immediate inflation premium. This was important for equities and credit because the pressure on discount rates eased at the same time the energy tax on consumers and margins looked less severe.

Non-US policy kept a floor under the hawkish debate. The ECB's rate-hike decision guide framed the euro-area move as a response to inflation caused by the Iran war, with economists expecting the deposit rate to rise to 2.25% and forecasts likely to show higher inflation. European stocks could still rally because banks benefited and energy shares rose, but the policy message was clear: central banks were prepared to react if energy moved into broader prices. In the US, June 11 was therefore a tactical bull rally in duration, not a strategic easing cycle.

## Supply, term premium, and liquidity

The supply signal came through the auction sequence rather than a broad long-end conclusion. The US 30-year auction was soft enough to produce a brief yield setback, but the macro rally then overwhelmed it. That sequence matters. It means June 11 should not be described as proof that long-end supply was easy to absorb. The supported point is narrower: even with a weak auction, lower oil and lower Fed-hike pricing were powerful enough to pull duration buyers back in. UST30Y fell 8 bp to 4.95%, but the reason was macro repricing, not a positive auction read.

Global supply and market-function facts were mixed. Thailand's curve had steepened to the widest in emerging Asia, with 10-year bonds offering almost 110 bp over 2-year notes. That made long-dated Thai bonds attractive to some investors because the Bank of Thailand had a more dovish path than peers, but the attractiveness came after a sharp and unusual curve steepening. Indonesia was the opposite kind of signal: the 10-year yield rose 10 bp to 7.45% and Bank Indonesia had to allow yields to rise to stabilize the rupiah. That is a market-function story because policy was using yields as the price of defending the currency.

Corporate supply belonged mostly in credit, but two examples were relevant for market functioning. Hanuman Wind postponed its junk bond for a second time after failing to attract enough demand, showing that yield alone did not solve governance or parent-company concerns. Danantara, by contrast, raised $1.5 billion in a debut dollar issue and the bonds traded higher, though order books were not spectacular compared with many Asian transactions. These two deals define the day's supply lesson better than the Treasury auction does: markets were open, but not equally open to every borrower.

The liquidity conclusion should therefore be restrained. The day had a strong duration rally and better risk appetite, but the direct evidence did not say that auction demand, dealer balance sheets, or structural liquidity had improved. It said that lower oil could temporarily dominate supply friction. In a week with ECB tightening, fragile EM currencies, and high-grade corporate issuance, term premium remained sensitive to whether the oil rally truly reversed or merely paused.

Thailand's long-end valuation story belonged in this section because it was a pure curve and term-premium case. Ten-year Thai bonds offered almost 110 bp over 2-year notes, with the curve near the steepest since late 2022. Investors such as Eastspring saw value after a sharp and unusual steepening, helped by a more dovish Bank of Thailand path than regional peers. That was not a US supply fact, but it showed where global duration buyers were willing to add risk: markets with attractive curve compensation and a policy path that looked less hawkish than Indonesia or Europe.

Indonesia showed the other side of term-premium adjustment. The 10-year yield rose to 7.45%, near the highest since 2022, while the rupiah stayed under pressure and officials let yields rise to defend the currency. Bank Indonesia's stance made higher yields a tool for restoring foreign participation, not merely a market accident. In a global FI report, that is important because it separates liquidity repair from price relief. A market can have higher yields and still be trying to rebuild confidence rather than signaling improving fundamentals.

Bank and corporate issuance added evidence that market access was open for cleaner credits. Citigroup's banking unit sold $6.25 billion of high-grade debt in its first such offering of the year, while Lloyds used the Samurai market where Japanese demand for shorter maturities and wider spreads had been strong. Those deals stood apart from Hanuman's failed junk-bond attempt. The supply conclusion is that investors were providing balance sheet to issuers with recognizable quality, rating support, or attractive market structure, while rejecting governance risk even when the coupon looked high.

Indonesia was the best example of why that distinction mattered. Danantara sold $1.5 billion of debut global dollar bonds in two equal tranches, and the securities traded higher, giving the government a visible capital-market success after the prior rout. At the same time, Indonesian local bonds resumed selling off, with the 10-year yield near its highest level since 2022 as investors waited for more policy clarity. Successful external issuance therefore did not mean local-market confidence was fully repaired. It meant the market was still open for a state-linked issuer with enough spread and a clear story, while local duration still needed a higher risk premium.

Thailand and Japan added two different supply signals. Thai 10-year bonds offered roughly 110 bp over two-year bonds, the steepest curve in emerging Asia, making long bonds attractive to investors willing to own a more dovish local path. Japan, by contrast, had just seen poor long-end demand and faced a BOJ meeting without Ueda at the table. The common point is that long-duration buying was conditional: investors wanted either a steep enough curve, a credible policy path, or a macro shock large enough to overwhelm weak auction optics.

## Credit conditions

Credit improved on total return, but the better interpretation is quality tiering inside a better tape. LQD rose 0.85%, HYG 0.59%, JNK 0.57%, and EMB 0.96%. Falling Treasury yields mechanically helped those prices, so the ETF moves alone cannot be translated into spread tightening. But the credit news flow was more constructive than the prior day because risk appetite reopened, equities rose, and volatility fell. The question was not whether credit was closed. The question was which borrowers could use the window.

Danantara was the clearest positive market-access example. Indonesia's sovereign wealth fund sold $1.5 billion of debut global dollar bonds in two equal five- and 10-year tranches, and both traded higher in Asian trading. The order books were not described as spectacular, but the deal still gave President Prabowo's government a concrete capital-market success after the broader Indonesian rout. That mattered because Indonesian local bonds and the rupiah were under pressure. Successful hard-currency issuance did not erase domestic market fragility, but it showed that credible quasi-sovereign paper could still clear.

Hanuman Wind showed the other side. The issuer postponed a junk bond offering again even though the notes offered a yield in the mid-to-high 9% area. Investors were not comfortable with parent governance, and rising US Treasury rates for 15-year paper made the deal less attractive. This is the core credit lesson: when Treasury yields and governance risk both rise, a high coupon is not enough. Credit demand was strong for the right structures, not unconditional.

AI financing remained a major credit theme. The proposed AI exposure disclosure bill reflected concern that chipmakers, data centers, cloud providers, hyperscalers, and direct-lending lenders were building a debt ecosystem around AI capex. Apollo and Blackstone had finalized a $35 billion financing package for Anthropic infrastructure, one of the largest direct-lending deals in history. That does not make every AI borrower weak, but it does mean debt investors have to separate tenant strength, project structure, and refinancing risk. SpaceX, Oracle, Anthropic, and related software names showed how quickly equity enthusiasm can interact with bond and loan markets.

For spread interpretation, June 11 was constructive but not complacent. Oracle bonds rallied and CDS protection costs fell in separate news, Danantara cleared, and equities rallied hard. At the same time, Travelodge debt stress, Hanuman's failed deal, and Indonesian local-market fragility showed that weaker borrowers still faced a high bar. The credit conclusion rests on that distinction: the oil relief rally improved the market window, but it did not flatten borrower quality.

Oracle was a useful reminder that equity and credit can react differently to the same issuer. Its shares were under pressure, yet parts of its bond curve and CDS improved after management signaled discipline around additional issuance. That is the kind of detail a fixed-income tape should capture. Credit investors were willing to reward a credible funding outlook even when equity investors were skeptical about near-term growth. It reinforces the theme that borrower behavior and debt supply plans mattered as much as broad beta.

Travelodge supplied the opposite example. A downgrade deeper into junk and rising bearish positioning around its notes reflected concern that weak operating performance and limited deleveraging capacity would make refinancing harder. This was the same market in which higher-quality borrowers and some AI-linked names could still fund. The difference was cash-flow durability. Falling oil and lower yields improved the macro backdrop, but they did not turn a weak refinancing story into a strong one.

CoreWeave and Anthropic kept the AI financing channel central. CoreWeave-related funding had moved into euro and dollar junk bonds, while the Anthropic infrastructure package showed how direct lending was scaling around AI capex. The constructive read is that investor demand was deep enough to finance data-center buildout across regions and formats. The risk is that the debt is increasingly project specific, rate sensitive, and dependent on assumptions about AI revenue growth. June 11's risk rally made those deals easier to discuss; it did not make their credit risk generic.

SpaceX also complicated the signal. A record-sized IPO and anticipation of investment-grade debt access showed that capital markets were receptive to premier growth issuers. But the same day also carried skepticism about valuation and free cash flow around some high-profile names. For credit, the right conclusion is not that AI and space-related financing were uniformly strong. It is that the market was separating franchise quality and collateral from weaker governance, stretched leverage, and unproven cash-flow conversion.

The weaker side of credit was visible in borrowers that could not rely on the improved macro tape. Hanuman Wind pulled a junk bond again after governance concerns at its parent kept buyers away. Travelodge was downgraded by Moody's as debt concerns mounted for the UK hotel chain. Zimbabwe sought a $150 million AfDB loan to address arrears. Those examples matter because they prevent the June 11 risk rally from being overread. Falling oil and lower yields helped market beta, but governance risk, leverage, and sovereign arrears still decided whether capital was available on acceptable terms.

Large-bank issuance gave the other side of the market. Citigroup raised $6.25 billion in the year's first high-grade bank bond sale, a sign that high-quality borrowers could still access size when the macro tape improved. That is a different credit signal from Danantara, Hanuman, or Travelodge. It tells readers that June 11 reopened windows at the top of the quality spectrum first, while weaker borrowers still needed concessions, balance-sheet repair, or official support.

## Global duration, EM hard-currency credit, and cross-market confirmation

Global duration gave the US move credibility but also showed why the relief was conditional. The ECB was tightening because energy inflation had broadened, not easing because oil fell for one day. South Africa had raised rates to 7% in May to anchor expectations, and its business community was already questioning the growth cost. Indonesia was allowing yields to rise to defend the rupiah. Thailand's long end looked attractive only after the curve had steepened sharply. In other words, global duration had opportunities, but those opportunities came from dislocations created by inflation and currency pressure.

The strongest cross-market confirmation came from oil and equities. Brent's drop below the prior day's levels gave investors a reason to rebuild risk exposure. European stocks snapped a losing streak, US equities rallied, and semiconductors surged. VIX fell more than 12%. That broad confirmation is why the Treasury rally should not be described as a recession trade. It was closer to an oil-tax relief trade: lower oil reduced inflation pressure and supported growth-sensitive assets at the same time.

EM hard-currency credit was selective. Danantara's deal traded higher, while Indonesia's local-currency bonds and equities were still fragile. Thailand attracted long-end buyers because valuation had improved, but its relative appeal depended on the Bank of Thailand being more dovish than peers. Zimbabwe, Turkey, South Africa, and Brazil each had their own inflation or funding issues in the day's coverage, showing that EM policy was not moving in one direction. The broad rule was simple: oil relief helps EM, but countries still need credible policy or sufficiently high yields to attract capital.

The dollar and gold were not the main story. DXY fell only 0.09%, while gold slipped 0.44%. That restrained response suggests the market was not merely dumping havens. Instead, it was rotating out of the worst inflation-tail risk. The stronger evidence was in breakevens, equities, VIX, and the front end. The day therefore closed with a cleaner cross-market message than June 10: lower oil allowed duration and risk to rally together, but central banks outside the US were still proving that energy inflation could force tightening if the relief failed to persist.

Indonesia and Thailand were the clearest Asian duration contrast. Thailand's curve steepening created long-end value because inflation and growth allowed the central bank to be more patient. Indonesia had to tolerate higher yields to stabilize the rupiah and lure foreign capital. Both were EM duration stories, but they pointed in opposite directions. That distinction matters because June 11's global rally could not be applied mechanically across EM. Duration worked where valuation and policy credibility aligned; it remained fragile where currency defense dominated.

The ECB made Europe the hawkish anchor. It raised the deposit rate to 2.25%, lifted inflation projections, and warned that price pressure was spreading beyond energy. Officials were already leaving open additional moves. European equities could rise because banks benefited and the market liked the end of a losing streak, but the fixed-income message was harder: Europe was willing to trade off growth to defend inflation credibility. That limited how dovish the global duration story could become even with oil lower.

South Africa, Turkey, Brazil, and Zambia showed the same policy-differentiation theme. South Africa's rate hike was being questioned by business groups because growth was weak, but the central bank was focused on expectations. Turkey kept policy at very high levels and used a costlier funding channel while inflation remained elevated. Brazil inflation moved above the top of its target range before a policy decision. Zambia had strong local-bond returns after progress on a dollar-bond buyback and a rally in the kwacha. EM performance therefore depended on the interaction of inflation, FX, and credibility, not simply on global risk appetite.

The dollar and gold supported the relief interpretation without removing caution. DXY slipped and gold fell, consistent with lower haven demand. But the week-to-date VIX level was still elevated even after the one-day collapse, and oil was still high enough to keep policy debates live. The global cross-market message was positive on the day and still unsettled over the week. That is why the note should call June 11 a relief rally, not a regime change.

Europe made the relief rally more complicated. The ECB raised rates for the first time since 2023 because the Iran-war inflation shock had become broad enough to require a policy response. That happened on the same day that oil relief helped U.S. duration and risk assets. For a global fixed-income reader, that divergence is the point: one market can rally because the marginal energy headline improves, while another central bank still has to respond to the accumulated pass-through. The ECB decision kept the global policy floor higher even as the U.S. curve rallied.

Turkey and South Africa showed similar constraints. Turkey held rates after earlier tightening, but reserves had fallen and the central bank was still managing war-related pressure. South African business groups questioned the growth cost of hikes even as more tightening was being discussed. These were not clean easing stories. They were economies trying to balance currency, inflation, and activity at the same time. That is why the June 11 U.S. rally was a relief trade rather than a global easing cycle.

## Market implications and watchpoints

The first watchpoint was whether the oil decline would hold. The whole June 11 rally depended on the market believing that the Iran escalation premium had fallen. If WTI and Brent stayed lower, breakevens could remain under pressure, Fed-hike pricing could drift later, and equities could keep rotating toward growth and semiconductors. If crude reversed, the same tape could quickly rebuild hawkish Fed probability and reprice credit spreads wider.

The second watchpoint was Warsh's first Fed meeting. The market had moved away from a fully priced December hike, but it had not embraced easing. Warsh's approach to communication mattered because he had signaled less enthusiasm for forward guidance. A less-guided Fed would raise uncertainty around the reaction function, especially with producer prices hot and oil volatile. Rates markets needed to hear whether the Fed saw oil as a temporary tax or a risk to inflation expectations.

The third watchpoint was the ECB. Its hike made Europe the cleanest case of a major central bank responding to the Iran-war inflation shock. If the ECB signaled additional hikes, US rates could not ignore it even if oil fell for a session. If Europe emphasized optionality and oil kept falling, the global duration rally would have more support. That distinction mattered for the dollar, EM local rates, and cross-border demand for Treasuries.

The fourth watchpoint was primary-market selectivity. Successful deals such as Danantara and resilient high-grade borrowers suggested markets were open. Hanuman Wind's postponement and weaker borrower stress showed that access was conditional. The next confirmation would come from whether issuers with governance, leverage, or cash-flow questions could clear without paying punitive concessions. June 11 improved the backdrop; it did not remove the late-cycle credit filter.

One additional watchpoint was whether the better primary-market tone would extend beyond the cleanest names. Citigroup and Lloyds could issue because bank credit and Samurai demand were functional. Danantara could clear because quasi-sovereign paper offered a political and valuation story. Hanuman could not clear because governance risk overpowered yield. If the next session showed that only top-tier or special-story borrowers had access, the credit rally would remain narrow. If weaker borrowers cleared without large concessions, the market would be confirming a broader easing of financial conditions.

Another watchpoint was whether Asia's EM curves stabilized. Thailand's long-end appeal and Indonesia's yield defense were both important, but for opposite reasons. If Indonesian yields kept rising and the rupiah stayed weak, the global rally would have an EM funding leak. If Thailand continued to attract duration and Indonesia's dollar bond access improved, the market could treat the oil drop as a genuine relief for Asian fixed income. The next day's EM price action was therefore a necessary check on the US and European risk rally.

The third additional test was whether ECB hawkishness would spill back into US rates. The ECB had become the first major central bank to hike in response to the war-driven inflation shock. If European officials kept stressing broadening price pressure, US rates could not treat oil relief as a permanent dovish signal. If Europe shifted toward optionality because oil kept falling, the US front end would have more room to hold the June 11 rally.

The third watchpoint was whether credit beta could keep improving without help from lower yields. June 11's total returns were boosted by the Treasury rally. If UST5Y and UST10Y stopped falling, spreads would have to do more of the work for LQD, HYG, JNK, and EMB to keep rising. That made new-issue quality and secondary liquidity more important than the ETF close. A healthy follow-through would show strong demand for high-grade banks, better EM sovereign-linked borrowers, and carefully structured leveraged deals at the same time. A weak follow-through would leave the day as mostly a duration-led rebound.

methodology_note: Original market synthesis from checked prices and fixed-income news developments.
